confirmed.title: split_full_reply leaks a preferences_md 'text | https://…' fragment as a bogus company seed when the config JSON is non-strict (trailing comma / comments)
confirmed.detail:
split_full_reply (ui/ai_setup.py:490-517) locates the config object via _best_config_object, which has a TOLERANT fallback: when no brace-balanced substring passes strict json.loads (e.g. the LLM emitted a trailing comma or // comment — exactly what the fallback exists for, per its own docstring at :287-294), it repairs the JSON via claude_bridge._extract_json and returns the repaired dict. split_full_reply then calls _locate_object_span(src, obj) to get the config block's char span so seed-line scanning can EXCLUDE the config interior (the pipe-inside-JSON guard). But _locate_object_span (:538) pins the span by strict json.loads(text[start:i+1]) == obj on the RAW (unrepaired) substring — which can never equal the repaired obj — so it returns None. With config_span=None, the exclusion at :514 is skipped, and any preferences_md value containing a `| https://…` substring matches _SEED_LINE_RE and is emitted verbatim into seed_text.

Reproduced: a ````json` block whose object has a trailing comma AND preferences_md="See portfolio | https://example.com/me here." yields seed_text = '  "preferences_md": "See portfolio | https://example.com/me here.",'. In the /ai-setup/apply-full chained first_run job that line flows to apply_seed_lines(...probe=True), where parse_line derives a 'company' from the JSON-key fragment and probes https://example.com/me live, polluting the shared companies.json registry with a garbage entry (and mis-reporting seed_count). Strict JSON with the same preferences_md pins the span correctly and does NOT leak (verified by contrast), so this is isolated to the non-strict-JSON fallback path. Trigger is narrow (non-strict JSON AND a `| http` inside preferences_md) but both halves are realistic LLM output, and trailing commas are common enough that the fallback was purpose-built for them. Existing test test_split_full_reply_pipe_inside_json_is_not_a_seed (:349) does not catch it: its preferences_md pipe has no `http` after it, so _SEED_LINE_RE wouldn't match even without the span exclusion.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Make _locate_object_span tolerant the same way _best_config_object is: compare the candidate substring using the same tolerant parse (route the substring through claude_bridge._extract_json / the same normalization) before the == obj check, OR have _best_config_object return the (start,end) span alongside the object so split_full_reply doesn't re-derive it with a stricter parser. Alternatively, when config_span is None but config_text is non-empty, fall back to excluding any line that lies within the first ````json ... ```` fence.

verdict.reason: CONFIRMED via empirical reproduction. Mechanism holds exactly as described: _normalize_pasted (ui/ai_setup.py:247-257) strips // and /* */ comments but NOT trailing commas, so a trailing-comma JSON survives normalization in `src`. _best_config_object(src) fails its strict brace-walk json.loads on the trailing comma and falls to the tolerant claude_bridge._extract_json fallback, which repairs the JSON via _strip_trailing_commas and returns a REPAIRED dict (no trailing comma). _locate_object_span (ai_setup.py:522-543) then does strict `json.loads(text[start:i+1]) == obj` on the RAW substring (still containing the trailing comma) at line 538 -> always raises JSONDecodeError -> returns None. With config_span=None, the exclusion guard at ai_setup.py:514 is skipped, so a preferences_md value containing `| https://…` matches _SEED_LINE_RE and leaks into seed_text. Reproduced: trailing-comma JSON with preferences_md='See portfolio | https://example.com/me here.' yields seed_text='  \"preferences_md\": \"See portfolio | https://example.com/me here.\",' while the byte-identical STRICT-JSON contrast yields seed_text='' (no leak) — isolating the bug to the non-strict fallback path. Downstream harm confirmed: webui/api/onboarding.py:252 calls split_full_reply then :291 feeds seed_text into apply_seed_lines(probe=True) in the autorun first_run job; parse_line (scrape/ats_detect.py:325) on the leaked fragment returns a NON-None CompanyEntry(name='\"preferences_md\": \"See portfolio', ats_type='direct', slug='https://example.com/me'), a garbage entry that gets probed live and saved to the shared companies.json, and inflates seed_count. The existing test test_split_full_reply_pipe_inside_json_is_not_a_seed (test_ai_setup.py:349) does NOT catch it (its pipe has no http after it, so _SEED_LINE_RE wouldn't match anyway, and it uses strict JSON so the span pins correctly). NOT registered in brain/techdebt-register-2026-07-05.md (only unrelated ai_setup/onboarding hits: dead-code, ui-import-layering, bundle size) and NOT in docs/KNOWN_ISSUES.md (zero matches). Severity correctly minor: trigger requires two jointly-narrow-but-individually-realistic LLM-output conditions (non-strict JSON AND a `| http` inside preferences_md), and the P0-6 verified-by-default gate would fail the bogus probe and flag it unverified rather than scrape it — but it still pollutes the tracked registry and mis-reports seed_count, a genuine correctness defect.

confirmed.title: Finished JobRunner thread never releases its cached WAL connection, leaving tracker.db locked (WinError 32 root cause)
confirmed.detail: The JobRunner runs each engine job on a daemon thread named job-*. That thread calls init_db()/inbox_add_many() -> tracker/db.py get_conn(), which caches a WAL connection in the module _registry keyed by the thread ident. When the job completes, neither JobRunner._run's finally (webui/jobs.py:308-317, which only releases the exclusive slot) nor daily_run_core.run_ingest's finally (daily_run_core.py:86-88, which only unpins the project) closes that connection. It is reclaimed only by _sweep_dead_threads_locked (tracker/db.py:156), which fires solely from _register_current_thread on a NEW-thread cache miss. If no fresh-thread get_conn happens between job completion and a project-directory delete (the symptom observed tonight after a first_run job), tracker.db + its -wal/-shm sidecars stay open and the delete fails with [WinError 32] until the process restarts.
confirmed.fix_sketch: In JobRunner._run's finally (after job.finish), call tracker.db.close_all_connections() or a thread-scoped release, OR run a proactive _sweep when any engine job leaves the running state, so the DB lock is dropped deterministically on completion rather than on the next incidental cache miss.

verdict.reason:
CONFIRMED and reproduced. All load-bearing claims verified against the actual code, plus an empirical WinError 32 repro.

Mechanism (verified in source):
- webui/jobs.py:284-322 — JobRunner._run runs the job on a daemon thread named job-{kind}-{id}; the finally (L308-317) ONLY clears self._exclusive_active. No connection close, no .join(), and jobs.py has zero references to close_all_connections/close_db/release_for_restore.
- daily_run_core.py:86-88 — run_ingest's finally only restores sys.argv and calls workspace.unpin_active(). No connection close.
- tracker/db.py:210-226 get_conn() caches one WAL sqlite3.Connection per thread in _tlocal AND registers it in module _registry keyed by thread ident (L166-173 _register_current_thread). Connections are opened check_same_thread=False with PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL (L176-191), so on Windows they hold tracker.db + -wal/-shm open.
- tracker/db.py:156-164 _sweep_dead_threads_locked() is the ONLY path that closes a dead thread's cached connection, and it has exactly one call site: db.py:169 inside _register_current_thread — i.e. it fires only on a NEW-thread get_conn() cache miss, exactly as the finding states.

Empirical proof (py -3.12 repro against the real module): a daemon thread named job-first_run-* calls get_conn(), writes, then returns/dies (is_alive()==False), yet _registry still holds 1 entry and a gc.collect() does NOT close it (registry holds a strong ref). Deleting the db then fails with the exact symptom: '[WinError 32] The process cannot access the file because it is being used by another process' on tracker.db-wal, -shm, and tracker.db. After close_all_connections() the registry drains to 0 and the files delete cleanly — proving the dead-thread cached connection was the lock.

Not already registered: docs/KNOWN_ISSUES.md:110 is a historical 'Fixed since first written' note about an EARLIER, different get_conn leak (the pre-S38 per-call fresh-connection leak), not this dead-JobRunner-thread cached-connection-at-delete bug. brain/techdebt-register-2026-07-05.md:123 is about tracker/db.py being a 1,897-line god-object (structure debt) and only mentions connection lifecycle in passing. Neither registers this specific finding.

Severity: keeping MAJOR, not critical. The bug is real and reproducible and there is genuinely no connection-release call on the job-completion or delete path. But observable impact is bounded/self-healing in the live web app: per-request Flask/webui server threads call get_conn() constantly, and any such new-thread cache miss triggers _sweep_dead_threads_locked() which reclaims the orphan; atexit close_db() (db.py:320) also releases on process exit. The durable [WinError 32] therefore requires the specific narrow condition the finding names — an external/manual project-folder delete on a mostly-idle process shortly after a first_run job, with no intervening fresh-thread get_conn(). Real correctness/robustness defect worth fixing (e.g. release the thread's connection in JobRunner._run's finally, or sweep on job completion), but its blast radius is a narrow timing window rather than a guaranteed data-loss/crash, so major is the right tier.

confirmed.title: harvest() swallows all inbox-scoring exceptions and still returns HTTP 200 with inboxed:0 -> silent drop of hand-picked jobs
confirmed.detail: The inbox-routing block (score_jobs / load_user_config / preferences.load / inbox_add_many) is wrapped in a bare `except Exception as e: print(...)` at browser_receiver.py:518-520. On any failure (malformed preferences.json, an effective_keywords bug, a DB hiccup) execution continues to `return jsonify({received: N, inboxed: 0})` with HTTP 200. The extension/web UI renders this identically to 'N received, 0 genuinely new', so the user believes their hand-picked browser-harvested postings were triaged when every one was silently dropped; the only diagnostic is a console print the exe/web user never sees. Contradicts the project's stated 'no silent drop / surface source failures' philosophy.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Include an error signal in the JSON response (e.g. inbox_error: str(e) or a failed flag) so the UI can surface that inboxing failed, instead of reporting a clean inboxed:0 indistinguishable from success.

verdict.reason:
CONFIRMED and NOT already registered. Code matches exactly: scrape/browser_receiver.py:484-520 wraps the entire inbox-routing block (load_user_config, preferences.load, effective_keywords, score_jobs, init_db, inbox_add_many) in a bare `except Exception as e: print(...)`. On any failure, `inboxed` stays 0 and execution falls through to `return jsonify({received:N, inboxed:0, ...})` with HTTP 200 (line 529). The only diagnostic is a console print the exe/web user never sees.

The rendering/silent-drop claim is verified as TRUE and is actually worse than stated: browser_ext/popup.js:129-132 renders the 200 response as `Sent N jobs, 0 new to inbox` — byte-identical to a legitimate "0 genuinely new" outcome — and then popup.js:135-137 takes the resp.ok branch and WIPES the harvested jobs from chrome.storage.local ({jobs: []}) and zeroes the badge. So the hand-picked postings are cleared from the extension's pending queue too; the only surviving copy is the on-disk HTML/CSV flat report (browser_receiver.py:474-475), not the scored inbox the user actually triages from. The user is shown a success message while every job was silently dropped.

The exception is genuinely reachable, not a dead branch: inbox_add_many (tracker/db.py:1290) performs real SQLite writes (sqlite3.OperationalError on lock/corruption/schema mismatch), and score_jobs (match/scorer.py:936) is a full scoring pipeline that can raise on malformed preferences.json / bad config state reaching effective_keywords. The failure is error-path-only (normal harvests inbox correctly), but when it fires the outcome is total silent loss of user-curated data presented as success.

NOT registered: brain/techdebt-register-2026-07-05.md mentions browser_receiver.py only for the unrelated :5001 tracker-link 404 (line 32) and a generic no-manifest file list (line 114); docs/KNOWN_ISSUES.md has no mention of harvest/inbox-routing/score_jobs swallow. Directly contradicts the project's stated "inclusion over precision / never over-drop / surface source failures" philosophy (CLAUDE.md). Severity held at major: error-path-only, but the swallow makes a total silent drop of hand-picked jobs undiagnosable in production, which is exactly the failure class the project singles out as unacceptable.

confirmed.title: SSE error-reconcile fires terminal handlers against a stale job after jobId changes mid-flight
confirmed.detail: The EventSource 'error' listener (lines 106-126) closes the stream and calls endpoints.jobStatus(jobId).then(...) — an async fetch. Its .then/.catch invoke hRef.current.onDone / onFailed and this hook's setStatus. But hRef.current is mutable (line 85, reassigned every render) and the effect only re-subscribes on [jobId] (line 131). Failure scenario: a Search/Inbox run drops its transport (error fires, close, status-fetch in flight), and before the fetch resolves the user starts a NEW run (parent passes a new jobId). The cleanup closes the OLD es, the new effect subscribes to the new job — but the in-flight jobStatus(oldId) promise still resolves and calls hRef.current.onDone(...)/onFailed(...) (now the CURRENT render's handlers, bound to the NEW job) plus setStatus(...). Result: the new run's console can flip to 'done'/'failed' or fire RunConsole.onDone (which invalidates inbox queries + toasts 'Inbox updated') on behalf of a job that is actually still running. Low probability (requires a drop + fast re-run) but a real cross-job state bleed. Fix: capture the jobId in the effect closure and ignore the resolved snapshot if it no longer matches, or abort the fetch in the effect cleanup (AbortController).
confirmed.fix_sketch: In the effect, snapshot `const myJob = jobId;` and guard the .then/.catch bodies with `if (myJob !== jobIdRef.current) return;`, or track an `alive` flag set false in the cleanup and bail when it's false before calling handlers/setStatus.

verdict.reason: CONFIRMED real cross-job state bleed; see full analysis in adjusted reason.

confirmed.title: 100-row inbox table re-renders in full on unrelated parent state changes (no row memoization)
confirmed.detail: InboxTable is a plain function component (line 593), not React.memo, and every row builds fresh closures + a fresh RowActions `actions` array each render (RowActions at line 833 rebuilds the 3-item array inline; each action wraps a Tooltip). The parent InboxTab holds many pieces of state that change independently of the row data: `running`, `consoleOpen`, `runJobId` (RunConsole streaming), `windowSize` (bumped by the IntersectionObserver on EVERY scroll near the sentinel — line 145), `selectedId`, `rangeSelected`, `detailOpen`. Any of these re-renders InboxTab, which re-renders InboxTable with new inline handler props (onSelect/onTrack/... are memoized, but visibleRows is a new slice ref and the table itself isn't memoized), re-rendering all ~100 visible <tr> rows and their Tooltip-wrapped IconAction clusters. Failure scenario: user scrolls the inbox (windowSize bumps) or a daily run streams (RunConsole re-renders bubble parent updates) → the entire visible table reconciles each time, visible as jank on a full 100-row window on low-end hardware. This is the row-level re-render hotspot (distinct from the already-fixed bundle-split #19). Fix: wrap InboxTable in React.memo and memoize per-row action arrays / extract a memoized <InboxRow> so unrelated parent state churn doesn't reconcile every row.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Extract a `const InboxRow = React.memo(function InboxRow({row, isSelected, isChecked, ...stableHandlers}) {...})` keyed by row.id, pass only primitives + stable callbacks, and memo the outer table; move windowSize/IO churn so it doesn't invalidate row props.

verdict.reason:
CONFIRMED and not already registered. Verified in webui/frontend/src/tabs/inbox/InboxTab.tsx: InboxTable is a plain function component (line 593, no React.memo), RowActions (833) rebuilds a fresh 3-item actions array inline (846-869) each wrapping a Tooltip via TriageActions/IconAction (row-actions.tsx:44-60), and rows render via an inline .map (707) with no extracted memoized InboxRow. The parent InboxTab holds independent state that changes without row data changing: windowSize (128), running/runJobId/consoleOpen (155-157), selectedId/detailOpen (120-121), rangeSelected (298). The high-frequency trigger is real and provable: the IntersectionObserver bumps windowSize on scroll near the sentinel (line 145 setWindowSize(...)), re-rendering InboxTab; because InboxTable is unmemoized it reconciles every visible <tr> plus its Tooltip-wrapped action cluster each time. Memoization would also be defeated anyway because onSelect is an inline non-memoized arrow (528-531) and visibleRows is a fresh useMemo slice ref keyed on windowSize (133). One nuance: the finding overstates the RunConsole streaming path — RunConsole keeps its SSE `lines` in local state (RunConsole.tsx:49) so line frames do NOT re-render the parent; only onTerminal->setRunning does. But the scroll/windowSize path alone substantiates the failure scenario. Not present in brain/techdebt-register-2026-07-05.md (finding #19 is bundle/lazy-load code-splitting, #21 is query-guard boilerplate — both distinct) nor in docs/KNOWN_ISSUES.md. Severity minor is appropriate: reconciling ~100 light rows is a perf smell / low-end-hardware jank, not a correctness bug, in a local single-user tool with windowing already capping visible rows.</reason>
<parameter name="adjusted_sev">minor

confirmed.title: AiSetupPanes prompt fetch has no cancellation — setState after unmount when the dialog closes mid-fetch
confirmed.detail: The prompt-fetch effect (lines 102-116) calls endpoints.aiSetupFullPrompt()/aiSetupPrompt() and, on resolve, setPrompt(r.prompt) / setPromptLoading(false) — with no `alive` flag or AbortController and no cleanup. AiSetupDialog renders AiSetupPanes only while open and remounts it via key={String(open)} (line 302), so closing the dialog UNMOUNTS the panes. Failure scenario: user opens 'Set up with AI', the prompt GET is slow, and they close the dialog (Esc / overlay click) before it resolves; when the fetch settles, setPrompt/setPromptLoading fire on the unmounted component — a React 'state update on an unmounted component' path (harmless warning in React 18, but a genuine no-cleanup leak of the pending work, and it masks the more serious pattern the other fetches in this file avoid, e.g. theme.tsx uses an `alive` flag). Same shape applies to onCopy's window.setTimeout(...setCopied) after unmount. Fix: guard the effect with an `alive` boolean set false in a cleanup return, or an AbortController passed to the fetch.
confirmed.fix_sketch: let alive = true; fetcher.then(r => { if (alive) setPrompt(r.prompt) }).finally(() => { if (alive) setPromptLoading(false) }); return () => { alive = false; };

verdict.reason: CONFIRMED and NOT already registered. Verified in webui/frontend/src/components/ai-setup-dialog.tsx: the prompt-fetch effect (lines 102-116) calls endpoints.aiSetupFullPrompt()/aiSetupPrompt() then .then((r)=>setPrompt(r.prompt)) and .finally(()=>setPromptLoading(false)) with NO alive flag, NO AbortController, and NO cleanup return (grep for `return () =>|AbortController|alive` in the file returns nothing). The unmount premise holds: AiSetupDialog (line 300) renders the panes as `{open && (<AiSetupPanes key={String(open)} .../>)}`, so when `open` flips to false (Esc/overlay/close) the `open &&` guard short-circuits and React unmounts AiSetupPanes immediately on re-render — independent of Radix exit-animation timing. A slow GET settling after close therefore fires setPrompt/setPromptLoading on the unmounted component. onCopy's `window.setTimeout(()=>setCopied(false),1600)` (line 126) is uncleaned in the same way. The comparator claim is accurate: theme.tsx:62/75 uses exactly the `let alive=true` / `return ()=>{alive=false}` guard the target lacks. NOT already registered: the techdebt register's only ai-setup-dialog.tsx entry (line 184-186) is about an unused eslint-disable comment at line 82, a different issue; no hit for unmount/AbortController/cancellation/setPrompt in brain/techdebt-register-2026-07-05.md or docs/KNOWN_ISSUES.md. Severity stays minor: React is v19.2 (package.json:31), which removed the unmounted-setState warning, so at runtime this is a silent no-op — no console warning, no crash, no meaningful leak (the promise resolves and is GC'd). It is a genuine missing-cleanup defect inconsistent with the repo's own convention, but produces no functional failure, matching the finding's own 'harmless' framing.
